discussion_comments.comment_body:
Good catch, addressed in 2bd5f3b44.

Replaced `len(tuple(active_requests))` with a single `active_count = len(active_requests)` snapshot that's reused in both the reduction math and the log message. `active_requests` is a `WeakSet` so `len()` is safe and O(1), and this also guarantees the two values can't drift.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
Great work on the benchmarks! The flag-based approach is a huge improvement for the common case.

One tweak regarding the copy.copy behavior: for subclasses overriding schedules_equal, the copy now happens unconditionally on every tick instead of only when the schedule changes.

We can restore the original behavior by simply moving it inside the condition:
```python
if self._has_custom_schedules_equal():
     if (self._last_schedule is None or
             not self.schedules_equal(self._last_schedule, self.schedule)):
         self._heap_invalidated = True
         try:
             self._last_schedule = copy.copy(self.schedule)  # only on change
         except Exception:
             self._last_schedule = None
             self._heap_invalidated = True
```

Also, two quick thoughts on edge cases:
- `old_schedulers`: For default schedulers, this now stays None forever. Previously it held a dict after the first tick. Any external introspection tools expecting a dict might break here.

- Flag reset placement: `_heap_invalidated = False` lives in `tick()`. If something calls `populate_heap()` directly (like tests/subclasses), the flag is left dirty and forces a rebuild on the next tick. It's probably safer to move the reset into `populate_heap()` itself.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
Fixed it - https://github.com/celery/celery/pull/9799/commits/ddf25b8ed7afcca3b2ace2deb038d524485683ed

Added `traceback_clear(exc)` to `handle_failure(`), following the same pattern used for `Reject`, `Ignore`, `Retry`, `Exception`, and `EncodeError` handling in the trace module.

This works because `traceback_clear(exc`) calls `tb.tb_frame.clear()` on each frame to release local variables while preserving the traceback chain for logging. Tests also cover this.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
There is no code duplication. The current implementation is similar to what Copilot suggested.

The traceback_clear function was defined and is called in the exception handler.

We have now added a comment in every place where this function is called to explain that it is used to prevent memory retention, making it easier for code contributors to understand.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
Isn't it more efficient to initialize a set before the loop and avoid the de-duplication process in the loop and instead convert the set to a list after it has been initialized.

```python
new_stamped_headers = {stamp for stamp in itertools.chain(headers, headers["stamped_headers"]) if stamp != "stamped_headers"}
headers["stamped_headers"] = list(new_stamped_headers)
```

That way you don't need the O(n) lookup to check if the stamped header already exists in the stamped headers list and instead, you'll get an O(1) lookup since that is one of the set's properties.
`itertools.chain` creates an iterator from multiple iterables.

@@ -103,17 +103,22 @@ def _reconnect_pubsub(self):
         self.backend.client.connection_pool.reset()
         # task state might have changed when the connection was down so we
         # retrieve meta for all subscribed tasks before going into pubsub mode
-        metas = self.backend.client.mget(self.subscribed_to)
-        metas = [meta for meta in metas if meta]
-        for meta in metas:
-            self.on_state_change(self._decode_result(meta), None)
+        if self.subscribed_to:
+            metas = self.backend.client.mget(self.subscribed_to)
+            metas = [meta for meta in metas if meta]
+            for meta in metas:
+                self.on_state_change(self._decode_result(meta), None)
         self._pubsub = self.backend.client.pubsub(
             ignore_subscribe_messages=True,
         )
+        # subscribed_to maybe empty after on_state_change
         if self.subscribed_to:
             self._pubsub.subscribe(*self.subscribed_to)
         else:
-            self._pubsub.ping()
discussion_comments.comment_body:
pubsub will receive an empty reply b'$0

\0x00...', this has no effect on celery's functionality, but causes extra connections and memory usage.  The reason is not to send a ping, but when redis using hiredis to receive data, each connection has a 64 bytes bytearray. Without sending ping, there are no these empty replies. </br>
 This problem was found when analyzing the high memory after running high-frequency tasks for a long time----there are many bytearray(b'$0

\0x00...') takes up some memory.  
The unexpected growth in memory is related to redis-py, not celery.
redis-py, I also made some changes [redis-py 3.5.3 less memory usage](https://github.com/uuip/redis-py/tree/3.5)

discussion_comments.comment_body:
```suggestion
        del pool_map[pid]
```

`dict.pop()` returns a value we don't need. We should use `del` instead as it will just remove the key.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
So now we set all headers every time we stamp? Even the non-stamped headers?
This is very inefficient. My design should allow us to perform only a partial update on the new headers that were stamped.
If we now return all headers instead, stamping can become an expensive operation.

+        headers = self._stamp_headers(visitor_headers, **headers)
+        return self.set(**headers)
+
+    def _stamp_headers(self, visitor_headers=None, **headers):
+        headers = deepcopy(headers)
+        if visitor_headers is not None:
+            visitor_headers = visitor_headers or {}
             if "stamped_headers" not in visitor_headers:
                 visitor_headers["stamped_headers"] = list(visitor_headers.keys())
-            _merge_dictionaries(headers, visitor_headers)
+            _merge_dictionaries(headers, visitor_headers, aggregate_duplicates=False)
+            headers["stamped_headers"] = list(set(headers["stamped_headers"]))
         else:
-            headers["stamped_headers"] = [header for header in headers.keys() if header not in self.options]
-            _merge_dictionaries(headers, self.options)
+            stamped_headers = [
+                header for header in headers.keys()
+                if all([
+                    header != "stamped_headers",
+                    header in self.options.get("stamped_headers", [])
+                ])
+            ]
+            if "stamped_headers" in headers:
+                headers["stamped_headers"].extend(stamped_headers)
+            else:
+                headers["stamped_headers"] = stamped_headers
+            for stamp in headers.keys():
+                if stamp != "stamped_headers" and stamp not in headers["stamped_headers"]:
+                    headers["stamped_headers"].append(stamp)
+            headers["stamped_headers"] = list(set(headers["stamped_headers"]))
+            _merge_dictionaries(headers, self.options, aggregate_duplicates=False)
 
         # Preserve previous stamped headers
         stamped_headers = set(self.options.get("stamped_headers", []))
-        stamped_headers.update(headers["stamped_headers"])
+        stamped_headers.update(headers.get("stamped_headers", []))
         headers["stamped_headers"] = list(stamped_headers)
-        return self.set(**headers)
+        for previous_header in stamped_headers:
+            if previous_header not in headers and previous_header in self.options:
+                headers[previous_header] = self.options[previous_header]
+        return headers
discussion_comments.comment_body:
I understand what you're doing now.
We're not copying all the headers but the stamped headers that were not touched by the current stamping, whether by providing key/values in the `headers` keyword arguments or by a visitor.
If we have a lot of stamps, that could still be costly to set them.

My concern is that if we already have a stamped header `foo` set to the value `True` and we're now stamping the `bar` header with the value `False` then according to this logic, we're setting `foo` again unnecessarily [here](https://github.com/celery/celery/pull/8045/files#diff-3a80ff45da16a11b96e26a63973d7d490187a68ddc1949e2dfd7fd090b208841R570).
This is less efficient than not touching `foo` at all.
I guess your comment should explain what happens during replacing that requires us to always set all stamped headers even if they were not touched by the visitor or by the kwargs.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
@thedrow 
> I guess your comment should explain what happens during replacing that requires us to always set all stamped headers even if they were not touched by the visitor or by the kwargs.

TBH I don't know. The new test cases are checking so many new edge cases that in the case of stamping with task replacement, this happened.

It is not perfect but it will require a lot of time investigating it just for the comment whereas the feature is still not completed, so if it's acceptable, I'd prefer to leave this code with the comment referencing the replace, but not investigate more at the moment as its too expensive for the sake of having a more detailed comment.
